During Your Appointment

One-tap mode — works with screen dimmed

Hand Signals
✋
Raise hand — Stop/Pause
Dentist will immediately pause everything
👍
Thumbs up — I'm okay, continue
Signal to proceed
✌️
Two fingers — Need a break
Short pause to breathe and recentre
🤏
Pinch fingers — Slow down please
Dentist will proceed more gently
You did it! 🎉
"Getting through a dental visit is genuinely brave."

Chair Sim
Chair Simulation 🪑
Practice makes calm
Simulate your entire appointment step by step.
Step 1 of 5
Step 1 — Arrival
Walking into the clinic
You walk in. The receptionist smiles. You check in and take a seat. The waiting area has soft lighting.
Step 2 — The Chair
Sitting in the dental chair
The chair is comfortable — like a recliner. The dentist greets you warmly and tells you exactly what they'll do.
Step 3 — The Sounds
Hearing the tools
The cleaning tool makes a gentle buzzing sound. It's not as loud as you imagined. Your hand signal is ready.
Step 4 — The Procedure
The cleaning begins
Water, suction, gentle pressure. The dentist narrates every step. You're breathing steadily. It's manageable.
Step 5 — Done!
You made it!
The chair rises. "Great job today." You rinse, sit up, and feel a wave of relief and pride. That's it.
Dental Emergency
Is this a dental emergency? 🚨
Answer a few quick questions and we will tell you exactly what to do.
Encouraging Words
Encouraging Words 💬
Tap arrows to cycle through 30 uplifting thoughts.
1 / 30
Dental Care
Your Mouth Deserves Love Too
No lectures. No judgement. Just the basics — made actually useful.
🌅 Morning (2 min)
✅ Brush for 2 full minutes (yes, actually time it)
✅ Use fluoride toothpaste — the cheap stuff works fine
✅ Clean your tongue (the real source of morning breath 💀)
✅ Rinse. Done. You're a hero.
🌙 Night (the important one)
✅ Brush before bed — non-negotiable
✅ Floss (yes we know, but even once a day helps more than never)
✅ Mouthwash is optional, not mandatory
✅ Don't eat after brushing. Goodbye chai at 11pm 🫖
⚡ The Bare Minimum (no guilt)
If you're exhausted, sick, or just had a rough day —
one brush at night is infinitely better than zero.
Imperfect consistency beats perfect streaks that collapse.
🕐 When to change your toothbrush
Every 3 months — or when the bristles look like they've been through a monsoon 🌧️
Also after any illness. Your brush remembers everything.
🔄 The Modified Bass Technique
Hold the brush at a 45° angle to the gumline.
Use small, circular motions — not aggressive scrubbing.
Go tooth by tooth. Don't rush the back ones, they do all the real chewing.
⚡ Pro tip: Brushing too hard causes gum recession. Gentle is better.
🧵 Flossing (without hating it)
Use about 40cm of floss — wind most around your middle fingers.
Slide gently between teeth in a C-shape around each tooth.
Water flossers work too and are less annoying if you hate string floss.
Floss removes 40% of plaque that brushing misses.
👅 Tongue Cleaning
Use a tongue scraper or the back of your brush.
Start from the back, go forward — don't gag yourself.
Removes bacteria that cause bad breath far better than mouthwash.
Most bad breath comes from here, not teeth.
💧 Mouthwash: Worth it?
Fluoride mouthwash = yes, great for cavity prevention.
Antibacterial mouthwash = good for gum health.
Random "whitening" mouthwash = marketing mostly 👀
Use it after brushing, not instead of it.
🍎 What actually erodes enamel
Sodas, energy drinks, citrus juices — not one big villain, just frequent acid exposure.
Wait 30 min after acidic food before brushing. Brush immediately and you're scrubbing softened enamel.
Test what you know — no grades, just vibes.
💜
This is a safe space
If your teeth are not perfect — you are not alone.

In India, for decades, dentistry meant pain, judgment and shame. Children were dragged to clinics they feared. Adults avoided dentists for years because of one bad experience. The phrase "daat nikaalo" was a threat, not a choice.

None of that was your fault.
🟤 Brown spots on teeth?
Often staining from tea, coffee, or fluorosis from childhood drinking water — especially in small towns and villages. Not always decay. Not always preventable. Not something to be ashamed of.
🦷 Gaps between teeth?
Diastema is incredibly common, partly genetic, and has been considered beautiful in many cultures. It doesn't mean your teeth are "bad." It means you're human.
😬 Crooked or crowded teeth?
Often hereditary. Jaw size and tooth size don't always match — that's genetics, not neglect. You didn't fail anyone by having the mouth you were born with.
😰 Haven't been to a dentist in years?
The average Indian adult hasn't had a dental check-up in over 3 years. This is systemic. Cost, fear, access, stigma — all real barriers. You're not lazy. You're navigating a system that failed you.
🌱 SerenOra's promise
Your dentist here will not judge you for what your mouth looks like today.
Every visit is a starting point, not a report card.
Your comfort matters. Your pace matters. You get to go slow.

The journal is a place to write all of this down — the fear, the embarrassment, the questions you're afraid to ask out loud. Your dentist reads it and adjusts. No judgment, ever.
🌈
Little Smiles Guide
Everything parents and little ones need to know
🦷 Baby Teeth — When They Arrive
Primary (deciduous) teeth eruption ages
UPPER TEETH
Central Incisor
6–10 months
Lateral Incisor
9–13 months
Canine (Cuspid)
16–22 months
First Molar
13–19 months
Second Molar
25–33 months
LOWER TEETH
Central Incisor
6–10 months
Lateral Incisor
7–16 months
Canine (Cuspid)
17–23 months
First Molar
12–18 months
Second Molar
23–31 months
All 20 primary teeth usually present by age 3. Ranges are normal — every child is different!
🌟 Adult Teeth — When They Replace Baby Teeth
Permanent teeth eruption ages
First Molars ("6-year molars")
6–7 yrs
First permanent teeth — crucial, never replaced!
Central Incisors
6–8 yrs
Front teeth, upper then lower
Lateral Incisors
7–9 yrs
Beside the front teeth
Canines
9–12 yrs
The pointed ones — often last to settle in
Premolars
10–12 yrs
Replace the primary molars
Second Molars
11–13 yrs
"12-year molars"
Wisdom Teeth
17–25 yrs
Often need removal — not everyone gets all 4
🪥 Caring for Baby Teeth
🍼 Before teeth appear (0–6 months)
Wipe gums with a clean damp cloth after feeding. Gets the baby used to oral hygiene before teeth arrive. Start early — the habit forms before resistance does.
🪥 First tooth → start brushing
Use a soft infant brush. Under 2: just water. Ages 2–6: a smear of fluoride toothpaste (rice grain size). Over 6: pea-sized amount. Brush for them until they can tie their own shoelaces — that's the coordination level needed.
⚠️ Baby teeth matter — they're not throwaway
Baby teeth hold space for permanent teeth. If lost early to decay, permanent teeth drift and crowd. Decay can also affect the developing permanent tooth below. A cavity in a baby tooth needs treatment.
🍬 Avoid nursing bottle caries
Never put a baby to sleep with a bottle of milk, formula, or juice. Sugar pools around teeth for hours, causing rapid decay of front teeth. Water is the only safe bedtime bottle.
📅 First dentist visit
By the first birthday or within 6 months of the first tooth — whichever comes first. Early visits are brief and gentle. Prevention is always easier than treatment.
🦷 Wobbly tooth tips
Let baby teeth fall out on their own — gentle wiggling is fine, forcing is not. The permanent tooth pushes from below and the root dissolves naturally. If the adult tooth erupts without the baby tooth falling, see a dentist.
Calm Games
Mind Games
Evidence-based interactive exercises that rewire how your brain responds to dental anxiety.
🔬 Research-designed
Each game targets a specific anxiety mechanism: exposure, reframing, psychoeducation, or controlled breathing.

Parent Mode
Parent Mode
Do say
"The dentist is going to help keep your teeth strong and healthy."
Avoid saying
"It won't hurt at all!" — sets unrealistic expectations.
Do say
"If you want to stop, raise your hand and the dentist will pause."
Avoid saying
"Don't be scared" or "Be brave!" — invalidates feelings.

You did it!

Completing a dental visit is genuinely brave.

What happened in your brain today
Your amygdala fired — that's your brain doing its job. But you overrode it. Every time you do, the response gets a little smaller.
Anxiety Check
📊 MDAS — Modified Dental Anxiety Scale
A clinically validated 5-question scale used by dental researchers worldwide. Takes 90 seconds. Your score helps us personalise your experience.
Your Result
Post-Visit Check-In
How did your visit go? 🌿
3 quick questions. Your feedback builds longitudinal proof of what works.
Q1 of 3 — How was the overall experience?
Tap a star to rate
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
Q2 of 3 — Did your anxiety reduce?
Rate your anxiety during the visit (1 = calm, 10 = very anxious)
😌😨
1 — Very calm510 — Very anxious
Before-vs-During comparison:
Q3 of 3 — What helped most?
Breathing exercisesSound desensitisationChair simulationReframing cardsKnowing the stepsHand signalsKind dentistMusic during visitNothing helped much

Requires connection ⚡ Online

Serena AI Chat
Needs API connection — basic tips work offline
Online only
📅
Appointment Booking
Requires dentist clinic sync
Online only
Why this matters 📶
India has 650M+ people in areas with poor or intermittent internet. Making anxiety tools offline-capable could expand our addressable market by 3–5×. All session data syncs automatically when reconnected.

Reset Password

Create your account
👤 Signing up as Patient
🏥 Join a Clinic
Enter the join code given to you by your dentist's clinic.
🏥 Your Clinic
Give your clinic a name. A unique join code will be generated for your patients.
Enter your clinic's join code. An admin approval request will be sent to the clinic owner.
⏳ You'll have limited access until the clinic admin approves your request.
🏥 Your Clinic
Register a brand new clinic. A unique join code will be generated — share it with your dentists and patients so they can link to your clinic.
Your clinic already exists on SerenOra — dentists and patients are already registered under it. Enter the clinic's join code to link this admin account to it and manage all its dentists and patients.
💡 The join code is the same one your dentists used to register. Once linked, you'll see all dentists and patients under this clinic.
Secure & Private 🔒
Your account is protected by Supabase Auth. Patient records are only visible to authorised clinics using Row-Level Security.

What to do now
🌙 Menstrual Cycle Tracker
Dental pain sensitivity and healing are affected by your cycle. Tracking this helps you and your dentist choose the best time for procedures.
First day of last period
Average cycle length (days)
🤰 Pregnancy Mode
Pregnancy affects your oral health significantly — from morning sickness eroding enamel to pregnancy gingivitis. Activating this mode personalises your care and alerts your dentist to plan accordingly.
Which trimester are you in?
😬 Bruxism Morning Check-In
Teeth grinding causes silent damage over time. Log how you wake up — the pattern helps your dentist diagnose and treat bruxism early.
How did you wake up today?

Serena — AI Dental Guide

Your calm companion · Always here for you

Hi! I'm Serena, your calm dental companion. I know dental visits can feel scary — that's completely normal. Ask me anything about procedures, anxiety, or what to expect. I'm here to help! 🌿
I'm nervous Root canal? Injection fear Anxiety tips Toothache help Extraction? Calm down tips

🌅 Before Your Day
Set your intention. A few minutes here makes a measurable difference to how you handle stress later.

Today's Intention
⏱ Mid-Session Reset
Between patients. One minute to reset your body and mind before the next chair.
Quick Posture Check
✓  Shoulders down and back — not hunched
✓  Neck neutral, chin slightly tucked
✓  Weight evenly on both feet or seat
✓  Hands loose — unclench between patients
🫧
60-Second Breathing Reset

📎 Upload Existing Investigation Reports
Upload completed lab/radiology reports (PDF, JPG, PNG). They'll appear in the patient docket under Documents.

Add Custom Medicine
Additional Instructions
⚠️ Antibiotic Stewardship Reminder
Systemic antibiotics are not routinely indicated for irreversible pulpitis or localised dento-alveolar abscess in immunocompetent patients. Consider drainage and definitive treatment first. Prescribe only if there is systemic spread (fever, trismus, cellulitis, lymphadenopathy).
Source: SDCEP Drug Prescribing for Dentistry guidelines
